quotes.verbatim_quote:
At the close
of these sequences there is a "Cadence" which ends a musical thought. This is done by
progressing into the dominant chord of the tonality and from there into the tonic. We call
this the “Authentic Cadence".
quotes.why_load_bearing: Core definition of the chapter's central concept, establishing both the term and its harmonic mechanism (V to I motion).

quotes.verbatim_quote: Most popular music is written ineither eight or sixteen bar sequences.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the structural context in which cadences operate, anchoring the concept in common popular-music form.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The
difference between an introduction and an ending is that the introduction stops on the V
(Example 29). From this point you approach the first chord of the song. An ending
approaches the V and moves on to the I, which is the final statement.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives a precise, actionable rule for distinguishing introductions from endings based on whether the cadential motion stops on V or resolves to I.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The sequence does not have tobe eight or sixteen bars. There are introductions and end-
ings in which the "Authentic Cadence" appears that only consist of one or two bars.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Expands the application of the Authentic Cadence beyond standard phrase lengths, teaching that the concept applies at any structural scale.
